author: barnabasbusa
content:
Hey, a few logging improvement requests for the ERE server:

  1. Add slot number + block hash to logs
  Currently there's no way to correlate which slot/block the ERE server is proving. The EWS logs have slot=93 block_hash=0x7e26...59c6 but the ERE
  server just shows a proof ID with no slot context. It would be really helpful to include the slot number and block hash in the PROVE SUMMARY and
  Received 'Prove' request log lines.

  2. Remove redundant "waiting for requests" log spam
  After every proof, there are 2-3 duplicate lines like:
  INFO ZisK Server waiting for requests on port 7878 for ELF '/root/.zisk/cache/a86a...cddd.elf'
  These flood the logs and make it hard to follow actual proof progress. One line on startup is fine, but repeating it after every proof (and between
   proofs) is noisy.

  3. Align proof ID with the EWS/zkboost proof_gen_id
  Right now it's impossible to correlate proofs across services. ERE logs:
  Proof ID: e666eaca33260488d68ebc1b0d233be57c26635b183ae82ee049db691ffd0173
  But EWS/zkboost use a UUID-based proof_gen_id for the same proof:
  proof_gen_id=e88ad92c-b860-4a61-a2a6-1bb00b133fa3
  Could the ERE server either log the proof_gen_id it receives from the request, or use a matching ID format so we can trace a proof end-to-end?

author: barnabasbusa
content:
4. Basic proof dashboard on the ERE HTTP server (port 3000)

  It would be great to have a simple web UI served on the existing ERE HTTP port (3000) that shows:
- Current status (idle / proving)
- Proof currently being worked on (slot, block hash, time elapsed)
- History table of completed proofs (slot, block hash, proof time, status)
- Basic stats (total proofs, average proof time, uptime)

  Nothing fancy — just enough to get a quick overview of what the prover is doing without having to tail logs. Similar to how spamoor has a simple
  dashboard on its HTTP port.

author: barnabasbusa
content:
5. execution_proofs endpoint returns 404 for valid blocks

  The /eth/v1/beacon/execution_proofs/{block_root} endpoint returns 404 even for blocks that have proofs submitted and available:

  curl -s https://bbusa-beast-lin.faun-cloud.ts.net/eth/v1/beacon/execution_proofs/0xd2ada3d7bf87bd6438f8804b585532205b55f50db73cab78cf245d8892cbce51

  {"code":404,"message":"NOT_FOUND: beacon block with root 0xd2ada3d7bf87bd6438f8804b585532205b55f50db73cab78cf245d8892cbce51","stacktraces":[]}

  EWS logs confirm the proof was submitted successfully for this block. Lighthouse is running with --activate-zkvm. Is the proof storage/lookup keyed
   differently than by block root?

author: taulepton_
content: How do you expect the network to progress in that context? The zk-attester nodes would enter optimistic mode and stop attesting.

author: taulepton_
content: Chain validity requires that every block in the chain's ancestry is marked as valid.

author: barnabasbusa
content: Will zk provers have to prove every thing block from genesis ? and catch up to head?

author: barnabasbusa

author: taulepton_
content: No, from the current finalised to the head

author: barnabasbusa

author: taulepton_
content: It's not safe from a consensus perspective to only prove isolated blocks. You have no guarantees that the parent is valid.

author: barnabasbusa
content: But for now, let’s focus on the blocks we can prove, and not on the ones we can’t

content: Even for optional proofs being able to fool ~10% of the network is dangerous

author: taulepton_
content: Better that zk attesters abstain from attesting then attest to things they're not sure of

author: taulepton_
content: Once I've finished an initial implementation on Lighthouse, I'm thinking of moving over to support the decentralised proving efforts. I'm looking into Ray as an orchestration engine. Keen to hear your thoughts regarding Kurtosis integration <@412614104222531604> https://github.com/ray-project/ray

author: barnabasbusa
content: instead of ere?

author: taulepton_
content: Yes

author: taulepton_
content: I think ere doesn't have any decentralised proving capabilities?

author: han0110
content:
Yes, the ere-server doesn’t support distributed proving, but some zkVMs support that (e.g. ZisK, SP1)

Also there is a mode for ere-server to forward the request to a distributed proving cluster with the same API, to make quick adaptation (but it adds latency, so ideally the EWS should just call the cluster)

author: taulepton_
content: Yeah, I'm thinking of using Ray to implement decentralised proving for a single-prover backend. If it's successful, then we can support other decentralised proving backends. I think Ray is a great orchestration engine and provides a good level of abstraction to support the different proving DAG's required for different provers. If Ray is superior to what prover teams currently use then maybe prover teams can maintain the ray DAG's in the future.

author: taulepton_
content: With Ray we can run multiple decentralised provers on the same cluster maximising resource utilisation.

author: barnabasbusa
content: ok let me know if you guys cooked something up

author: han0110
content: Yeah this makes sense, for OpenVM I actually did the cluster from scratch (with AI help), I think it requires pretty deep integration with the SDK, tho the complexity would probably heavily depend on the protocol (e.g. ZisK has shared challenge across chunks IIRC).

author: taulepton_
content: Can you elaborate on this please. What is it you did with OpenVM? Implemented your own decentralised proving cluster?

author: han0110
content:
> Implemented your own decentralised proving cluster?
Yes, since OpenVM doesn't have public cluster framework yet, I did a quick attempt on that, the strategy: N prover each proves 1/N chunks and aggregate into single internal node of aggregation tree, then send back to coordinator and pick one of worker to aggregate all internal nodes into the final root proof.This is straightforward and only use the public API, because the chunks themselves don't depend on each other. 

(for ZisK the flow will be more complicated, all chunks need to be committed together and used to derived the shard challenge, and continue proving, so one extra round of workers syncing would be required)

author: han0110
content:
It's real proof, this should be small block right?

> is there a way to skip blocks ? e.g if it takes more than 10s to create the proof, just skip it and go to the next one?
Currently no, ZisK doesn't support cancel a proving, but I'll try to find a way to do that.

author: han0110
content:
1. Ere server is guest program agnostic currently, so it doesn't know the slot number of block hash, but it makes sense for the Ere server to print more info for tracing, I think a quick fix is to let the client attach some metadata and the server print that, opened [eth-act/ere#303](https://github.com/eth-act/ere/issues/303) for tracking
2. The current logs we see are mostly from ZisK's prover (including the `PROVE SUMMARY`). ZisK's logger will need some code change to disable, opened [eth-act/ere#301](https://github.com/eth-act/ere/issues/301) for tracking. For other `ere-server-{zkvm}` we can control by setting `RUST_LOG` like `RUST_LOG=info,openvm=error` to hide them.
3. If 1. is resolved (client request with metadata), i think we can log the `proof_gen_id` as in Ere server

author: han0110
content: Can this be done in EWS? Which has more context about the actual guest being proved.
